what_it_does: Defines the motion face of the design system as a temporal theory of change, navigation, transitions, state, anchors, and page-to-page understanding routes.
observations: Frames motion as change over time rather than decorative animation.
Separates change into movement, transition, and state registers.
Grounds several laws in virtual-tour navigation practice and marks them as tours-direct.
Defines laws for no-teleport behavior, bounded change, anchors/changers, path-relative arrival, anticipatory verbs, spatial persistence, shared elements, boundary identity, and expert navigation.
Integrates prior motion guidance such as still-stands-alone, ambient budget, event motion, and easing as voice.
Records agent-extended amplifications ratified by Tim Geldard on 2026-06-11.
Connects transitions to address diffs, anchors to lowest common ancestors, page count to understanding distance, and cuts to information fault lines.
Ends with open threads for timing tokens, motion studies, and re-verification against tour materials.
standouts: Motion is treated as a scale-invariant law-set across relationship, artifact, page, interaction, and icon timescales.
The transition-is-the-animated-diff idea makes animation derivable from address comparison rather than hand-authored per case.
Arrival being relative to path applies virtual-tour orientation to both visual navigation and verbal slide/title transitions.
The ring law uses persistent boundaries to explain identity through state changes.
conventions: Uses manifesto-style prose with named laws in bold followed by parenthesized registry or evidence tags.
Marks evidence provenance inline with labels such as tours-direct, derived, deck-direct, and agent-extended.
Uses cross-registry references in backticks rather than defining those referenced records locally.
Uses a table for the three registers, then prose law blocks for detailed principles.
concerns: Tour-derived evidence is explicitly pending re-verification against primary tour materials.
Several referenced registry entries and JSON paths are not defined inside this file.
Section 6 is absent from the numbering.
notes: Brief this as the system's temporal grammar: motion is the readable, budgeted change between understandings, views, states, and boundaries.
questions: What content, if any, was intended for section 6?
Which primary tour artifacts will be used to re-verify the tours-direct laws?
How should temporal signatures become concrete duration or easing tokens?
Which parts of the change-intensity spectrum are intended to become encoded dials?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file presents itself as the temporal face and actually defines motion registers, laws, evidence notes, amplifications, and open threads for that face.
intention_signals: It seems intended to turn motion from animation taste into a governed temporal substrate for navigation, comprehension, interaction, and identity continuity.
novelty: It links virtual-tour point navigation to document/page/interface motion, treats transitions as animated address diffs, and uses lowest-common-ancestor boundaries as computed anchors.
contribution: Contributes the temporal substrate: registers of change, navigation-derived motion laws, anchor/changer mechanics, path-relative arrival, boundary identity through change, and derivation rules for transitions from structural addresses.
summary_for_embedding: This file defines the MOTION face as a temporal design theory where movement, transition, and state are governed by bounded change, anchors, path-relative arrival, shared continuity, boundary identity, and address-derived animated diffs.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to encode motion as a neutral, reusable law-set for how artifacts carry readers between understandings across pages, interactions, icons, and larger routes.
